# Browser-Based JavaScript/TypeScript Graph Database Solutions

Based on external research from GitHub repositories, here are 3 real solutions:

## Solution Comparison

| Solution   | GitHub Repository                                                | Stars | Storage                        | TypeScript     | Browser Support |
| ---------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------- | ----- | ------------------------------ | -------------- | --------------- |
| **LevelGraph** | [levelgraph/levelgraph](https://github.com/levelgraph/levelgraph) | 1,515 | IndexedDB ✅                   | ❌ (JS only)   | ✅ Full support |
| **Graphology** | [graphology/graphology](https://github.com/graphology/graphology) | 1,598 | In-memory (manual persistence) | ✅ Native      | ✅ UMD bundles  |
| **Graphlib**   | [dagrejs/graphlib](https://github.com/dagrejs/graphlib)           | 1,717 | In-memory (JSON export)        | ✅ Definitions | ✅ Browserify   |

---

## 1. LevelGraph ⭐ Best for Persistence

**GitHub:** https://github.com/levelgraph/levelgraph

**Overview:** RDF-style graph database using triple model (subject-predicate-object) with Hexastore indexing.

### Pros & Cons

| Pros                                                    | Cons                                                   |
| ------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| ✅ **Native IndexedDB support** for browser persistence     | ❌ **6x write amplification** (stores each triple 6 times) |
| ✅ **Fast pattern-matching queries** via Hexastore indexing | ❌ **No in-place updates** (delete + insert required)      |
| ✅ **Multiple API styles**: CRUD, Search, Navigator, Stream | ❌ **No native TypeScript** (JavaScript only)              |
| ✅ **LevelDB ecosystem** compatibility with plugins         | ❌ **Limited RDF support** (requires extensions)           |
| ✅ **Query planner** optimizes multi-pattern queries        | ❌ Write performance impacted by indexing overhead     |
| ✅ **Extensions available** for JSON-LD, N3/Turtle formats  |                                                        |

**Best for:** Applications requiring persistent client-side graph storage with complex querying.

---

## 2. Graphology ⭐ Best for Visualization

**GitHub:** https://github.com/graphology/graphology

**Overview:** Robust multipurpose graph library powering Sigma.js visualizations.

### Pros & Cons

| Pros                                                         | Cons                                                       |
| ------------------------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------- |
| ✅ **Native TypeScript support** with full type definitions      | ❌ **No built-in persistence** (manual IndexedDB/LocalStorage) |
| ✅ **Event-driven architecture** for reactive applications       | ❌ **Bundle size** can be large without tree-shaking           |
| ✅ **Comprehensive standard library**: algorithms, layouts, I/O  | ❌ **String key coercion** (non-string keys converted)         |
| ✅ **Versatile graph types**: directed, undirected, mixed, multi | ❌ In-memory only (no disk-based storage)                  |
| ✅ **Performance-optimized** iteration methods                   | ❌ Requires custom persistence implementation              |
| ✅ **Browser-specific modules** with web workers                 |                                                            |

**Best for:** Interactive graph visualizations and in-memory graph analysis.

---

## 3. Graphlib ⭐ Best for Algorithms

**GitHub:** https://github.com/dagrejs/graphlib

**Overview:** Directed/undirected multi-graph library with extensive algorithm support.

### Pros & Cons

| Pros | Cons |
|------|------|
| ✅ **Rich algorithm library**: Dijkstra, cycles, MST, traversal | ❌ **No native persistence** (JSON serialization only) |
| ✅ **O(1) complexity** for core operations | ❌ **O(\|V\|) complexity** for `sources()`, `sinks()` |
| ✅ **Compound graphs** for hierarchical structures | ❌ **High memory usage** for very large graphs |
| ✅ **TypeScript definitions** included | ❌ No built-in storage backends |
| ✅ **JSON serialization** for easy persistence | ❌ Limited to in-memory data structures |
| ✅ **Lightweight core** with modular design | |
<!-- table not formatted: invalid structure -->

**Best for:** Graph analysis algorithms and DAG operations.

---

## Recommendation for Your Tech Stack

Given your **Vite + Aurelia 2** frontend and **preference for client-side storage**:

1. **Primary Choice: LevelGraph** - Direct IndexedDB support aligns with your persistence requirement
2. **Alternative: Graphology** - If visualization is critical (works with Sigma.js), implement custom IndexedDB persistence
3. **Algorithm-Heavy: Graphlib** - If you need extensive graph algorithms but willing to handle persistence yourself

**Integration Note:** All three can be bundled with Vite. LevelGraph requires no persistence code, while Graphology/Graphlib need ~50-100 lines of IndexedDB wrapper code.
